coredata.dc:title: The self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots derived from Morus alba L. leaves for the rapid determination of tetracycline

Huang, Furong
coredata.dc:description: Morus alba L. (Mulberry), one of industrial crops, have been widely cultivated in China, mulberry leaves (Folium Mori), one of the main products of Morus alba L., is high yield and rich in nitrogen element, high-value-utilization of mulberry leaves is very important for agricultural economic development. Carbon quantum dots have been attracting extensive attention because of their excellent properties including tunable fluorescence, bio-compatibility, non-toxicity, high water-solubility, and ease of functionalization. Heteroatoms doping is an effective way to improve quantum yield and fluorescence performance of carbon quantum dots. In this work, the self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots was prepared from mulberry leaves, a nitrogen-rich, economic value-high and eco-friendly crops products, via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots exhibited certain stability, solubility and fluorescence intensity (quantum yield is 9.3%). With the introduction of tetracycline, the efficiency fluorescence quenching was observed, and an obvious color change can also be identified through naked eye. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is induced by internal filtering effect and static quenching. Based on this phenomenon, a rapid, naked-eye, and dual-readout assay for tetracycline detection was constructed and prepared as a fluorescent test paper. The linear range and detection limit of tetracycline were 0.158–40 μM and 158 nM, respectively. Other types of antibiotics, comment ions and chemicals show little influence on tetracycline detection. Furthermore, the constructed dual-readout assay was employed to detect tetracycline in river water with recovery range of 98.6–102.2%, relative standard deviation less than 2.54%. The constructed fluorescence probe show potential application to tetracycline detection in the real water sample.

The relationship between nitrogen content and fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs from mulberry leaves (mulberry leaves 1, 2, 3 and 4 were collected in Hebei, Anhui, Guangxi and Shandong Province, China, respectively) (b).
HRTEM image (inset: lattice spacing of 0.2 nm under HRTEM of the self-N-doped CDs.) (a), size distribution histogram (b), XRD patterns (c) and FT-IR spectra (d) of the self-N-doped CDs.
XPS survey of the self-N-doped CDs (a), C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) high resolution XPS spectra of the self-N-doped CDs.

UV–Vis, excitation and emission spectra of the self-N-doped CDs (a), inset are photos of the self-N-doped CDs in sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp; the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the self-N-doped CDs (b).

Fluorescence emission spectra of the self-N-doped CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of TC from 0 μM to 40.0 μM (a). A linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs solution and concentrations of TC added (b) (where F
0 is the fluorescence intensity without TC, and F is the fluorescence intensity with different concentrations of TC added). The stability of the self-N-doped CDs fluorescence probe sensing TC was detected by UV lamp with 365 nm (c). Effect of the other interfering substances on the fluorescent of the self-N-doped CDs. The listed substances are (1) blank, (2) TC, (3) SMS, (4) CPL, (5) ROX, (6) AML, (7) Na+, (8) Mg2+, (9) Ba2+, (10) Cd+, (11) Ca2+, (12) K+, (13) HCO3 -, (14) CO3 2-, (15) HPO4 2-, (16) H2PO4
, (17) NO2 -, (18) SO4 2-, (19) HA, (20) SA and (21) BPA (d) (the concentration of the other interfering substances is 2.0 M, and the concentration of TC is 20.0 μM). The test paper stability results (e). Photos of the sensing results of the test paper based on the self-N-doped CDs towards different antibiotics (f).

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of TC, excitation and emission spectrum of the self-N-doped CDs (inset are the photos of the self-N-doped CDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp before and after TC added) (a), the average fluorescence lifetimes of the self-N-doped CDs (b) and the self-N-doped CDs+ TC (c).
Detect TC in different concentrations with the test paper based on the self-N-doped CDs.
The fluorescence of self-N-doped CDs was quenched by tetracycline.
Conditions of preparation for the self-N-doped CDs.
Comparison of QY of CDs prepared from different biomass materials by hydrothermal method.
Comparison of other TC detection methods.
Analysis results of TC in the labeled samples based on the self-N-doped CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Morus alba L. (Mulberry), one of industrial crops, have been widely cultivated in China, mulberry leaves (Folium Mori), one of the main products of Morus alba L., is high yield and rich in nitrogen element, high-value-utilization of mulberry leaves is very important for agricultural economic development. Carbon quantum dots have been attracting extensive attention because of their excellent properties including tunable fluorescence, bio-compatibility, non-toxicity, high water-solubility, and ease of functionalization. Heteroatoms doping is an effective way to improve quantum yield and fluorescence performance of carbon quantum dots. In this work, the self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots was prepared from mulberry leaves, a nitrogen-rich, economic value-high and eco-friendly crops products, via a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots exhibited certain stability, solubility and fluorescence intensity (quantum yield is 9.3%). With the introduction of tetracycline, the efficiency fluorescence quenching was observed, and an obvious color change can also be identified through naked eye. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is induced by internal filtering effect and static quenching. Based on this phenomenon, a rapid, naked-eye, and dual-readout assay for tetracycline detection was constructed and prepared as a fluorescent test paper. The linear range and detection limit of tetracycline were 0.158–40 μM and 158 nM, respectively. Other types of antibiotics, comment ions and chemicals show little influence on tetracycline detection. Furthermore, the constructed dual-readout assay was employed to detect tetracycline in river water with recovery range of 98.6–102.2%, relative standard deviation less than 2.54%. The constructed fluorescence probe show potential application to tetracycline detection in the real water sample.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Morus alba L. (Mulberry) is the characteristic agricultural resource in Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region in China, which has a steady development trend in recent years. In 2020, Guangxi mulberry garden planting area has exceeded 0. 2 million hectares, ranked in the first in China, mulberry leaves (Folium Mori) output was also very large (Zhou, 2018). Mulberry leaves, as a kind of medical-food homologous plant rich in protein, vitamins and functional components, are mostly used as raw materials for silkworm rearing, traditional Chinese medicine and animal feed. The crude protein content is about 17–25% (nitrogen content is about 2.72–4%), mulberry leaves there are rich nutritional value (Sun et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2017). In recent years, expect for sericulture, the application value of mulberry leaves has been widely developed, such as used as drugs (Li et al., 2021), surfactants (Ahmadi et al., 2014), antioxidants and antibacterial agents (Yu and Shi, 2021).

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) is a new type of carbon material, which is generally less than 10.0 nm in diameter and was discovered by Xu et al. (2004). Because of its excellent properties including optical properties, bio-compatibility, non-toxicity, high water-solubility, and ease of functionalization (Yang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019), CDs have been application in cell imaging (Eskalen et al., 2020; Hou et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2020a, 2020b), photocatalytic (Hu et al., 2020; Jamila et al., 2020), and detection of metal ions and antibiotics (Qi et al., 2019). Citric acid (Ehtesabi et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020a, 2020b; Xing et al., 2020), cellulose (Liao et al., 2020), xylose (Xing et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018) and sucrose (Fu et al., 2021) have been used as carbon sources to synthesize CDs. Based on the biomass advantages of resources-rich, renewable, environment-friendly, and the purpose of costs-saving and high-value-utilization of biomass, researchers have been trying to synthesize CDs using biomass as raw materials. Rose (Feng et al., 2015), corn straw (Zhang et al., 2019), black pepper seed (Sharma and Yun, 2020), waste tea residue (Gunjal et al., 2019), hawthorn (Zhang et al., 2021a, 2021b), malva plant (Moaddab and Ghasemi, 2021), tulsi leaves (Kumar et al., 2017), coconut petiole residues (Gao et al., 2021), waste acorn cups (Xu et al., 2021) and bamboo leaves (Liu et al., 2019) have been successfully used to synthesize CDs. However, CDs from biomass often suffer from the weak fluorescence intensity. Surface passivation and heteratomic doping are the useful means to enhance the fluorescence of CDs. Nitrogen atom doping (Kumar et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2020a, 2020b; Zhang et al., 2021a, 2021b), copper atom doping (Guo et al., 2021), nitrogen and sulfur atom co-doped (Xing et al., 2020), nitrogen and cerium co-doped (Zhang and Fan, 2021), magnesium and nitrogen co-doped (Hu et al., 2021), silicon and nitrogen co-doped (Bai et al., 2019), and phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped (Kumar et al., 2017; Zan et al., 2018) has been widely used in the synthesis of CDs. Though, the doping of these atoms greatly increases fluorescence intensity of CDs, additional heteroatomic sources undoubtedly increase the cost and operation of the experiment. One of the effective methods to synthesis heteroatom doping carbon quantum dots is the self-doping of other atoms, such as N，P, etc., contained in the raw material itself.

Tetracycline (TC), one of antibiotic, is widely used in daily life, but it will remain in soil and water and is difficult to degrade naturally. Long-term living in an environment containing TC will pose a serious threat to biological health. Multiple methods have been developed for TC detection, such as high performance liquid chromatography (Feng et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2017), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Mookantsa et al., 2016), enzyme linked immunoassay (Wang et al., 2015), chemiluminescence (Townshend et al., 2005), capillary electrophoresis (Kowalski, 2008), and test paper colorimetry (Alfredsson et al., 2005). These technologies have high accuracy, but are not suitable for on-site inspection due to the need for sophisticated instruments and professional testing technicians. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a real-time, rapid and low-cost detection method for on-site monitoring of TC in the water environment.

Herein, the self-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (self-N-doped CDs) were fabricated from mulberry leaves, a kind of nitrogen-rich agricultural product, by one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared self-N-doped-CDs were used to construct “turn-off” fluorescence assay for TC detection. Based on the efficiency fluorescence quenching, a rapid test paper was fabricated for the naked-eye identify of TC and in real-time detection of emergencies cases.

Mulberry leaves were used in the work were collected in Hebei, Anhui, Guangxi and Shandong province, China. The mulberry leaves were washed to remove dust and any other impurities, dried at 50 °C for 24 h, then smashed and sieved with screen of 200 meshes, selected the mulberry leaves powder with particles of 0.075 mm as the precursor for production the self-N-doped CDs. TC (95%), streptomycin sulfate (SMS), chloramphenicol (CPL), roxithromycin (ROX), amoxicillin (AML) were obtained from Beijing Solaibao Technology Co., LTD. NaCl, MgCl2•6H2O, BaCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, CaCl2, KCl, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaNO3, NaNO2, Na2SO4, humic acidare (HA), sodium alginate (SA) and bisphenol A (BPA) are analytical pure grade chemical reagents and all of them were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. The deionized water in this work was produced by a microporous water purification system provided by Guangxi University, and the resistance of the deionized water is 0.0725 MΩ.cm detected by SLDS-1 conductivity meter.

The protein content in mulberry leaves was measured by automatic Kjeldahl apparatus (BUCHI KjelMaster K-375). The self-N-doped CDs were characterized as follows. The excitation and emission spectra were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). The UV–Vis spectra were carried out with the UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The structure and morphology was characterized through a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI TECNAI G2 F30, USA). The X-ray diffraction pattern was determined by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX2700A, Japan) in the range of 10°80°. The functional groups were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Perkin Elmer, USA). The elemental composition was analyzed by X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha Nexsa, USA). The changes of the fluorescence color and brightness were observed by three-way ultraviolet analyzer (Qiwei, China).

Pretreated mulberry leaves (2.2 g) which grown in Guangxi (used as both carbon and nitrogen sources) was added into 20 mL of deionized water, then stirred for 10 min, the mixture was transferred to teflon high pressure reaction kettle for reaction at 200 °C for 14 h, cooled down to room temperature and a brown mixture was obtained. Centrifuge the resulting mixture in a centrifuge at 4000 rpm for 15 min to separate solid from liquid. The obtained liquid was filtered by 0.22 µm membrane, further treated by a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove the small molecules, and finally dried in a freeze dryer for 24 h to obtain solid powder of the self-N-doped CDs. The self-N-doped-CDs (0.1 g) were distributed in 100 mL deionized water. The solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The solid powder of the self-N-doped CDs was kept in drying jar for later use. The preparation of the self-N-doped CDs is described in Scheme 1.

The preparation conditions of the self-N-doped CDs were optimized, including the source of mulberry leaves (Hebei, Anhui, Guangxi and Shandong), the dosage of mulberry leaves (1.2 g, 1.6 g, 2.0 g, 2.2 g, 2.4 g, 2.8 g and 3.2 g), hydrothermal reaction temperature (180 °C, 190 °C, 200 °C and 210 °C) and hydrothermal reaction time (9 h, 10 h, 11 h, 12 h, 13 h, 14 h and 15 h), other steps are the same as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the self-N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Photoluminescence ability of the self-N-doped CDs is expressed by QY. In this work, quinine sulfate was chosen as the reference solution when measuring QY. Quinine sulfate was added to 0.1 M sulfuric acid as a reference solution (fluorescence QY of 54% at 360 nm). The QY of the self-N-doped CDs was calculated by the Eq. (1) as follows (Moaddab and Ghasemi, 2021):
(1) Φs= Φr × (Is / Ir) × (Ar / As) × (ηs / ηr)2
Φ
r is QY of reference solution, I
s
and I
r are the excitation spectral integral area of the self-N-doped CDs and reference solution, respectively. A
s
and A
r are the absorbance of the self-N-doped CDs and reference solution, respectively. η
s
and η
r are the refractive index of the self-N-doped CDs and reference solution, respectively (both are 1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detected of TC by the self-N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor solution was prepared by adding TC solutions with a series of concentrations gradient into the self-N-doped CDs solutions. After 5 min, the change of fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs solution was measured and recorded by specific fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absorbance of the self-N-doped CDs solution was recorded by ultraviolet spectrophotometer after 20 min reaction at room temperature. And the color change of these solutions were observed and photographed under 365 nm UV lamp. The filter paper was soaked in the self-N-doped CDs solution (20.0 μM) for 10 min, then the soaked filter paper was taken out and dried at 25 °C. This procedure was repeated three times to prepare the fluorescent test paper. Fluorescence quenching of the self-N-doped CDs is shown in Scheme 2.

For the sake of verify the selectivity of the self-N-doped CDs on TC, other kinds of antibiotics such as SMS, CPL, ROX, AML, ions and chemicals that maybe exist in the water including Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, Cl
, HCO3
, CO3 2–, HPO4 2–, H2PO4
, NO3
, NO2 –, SO4 2–, HA, SA and BPA were investigated. The above substances were prepared into 2.0 M and added into 10 mL mixed solution (contain TC 20.0 μM). Fluorescence intensity of solutions before and after adding the interfering substances was measured and recorded by specific fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The water from Yongjiang River was used as real water sample to test TC. The collected water was centrifuged for 10 min in a 4000 rpm centrifuge, then filter with a 0.22 µm membrane. Firstly, the content of TC in the river water was detected, and then a series of concentrations of TC solutions were added into 10 mL river water. Changes of the fluorescence intensity of these solutions were observed with specific fluorescence spectrophotometer, and changes of the color of these solutions were observed by 365 nm UV lamp. The test paper based on the self-N-doped CDs was also used for detecting TC from river water, and changes of fluorescence color and brightness were observed by 365 nm UV lamp.

In this work, the effects of mulberry leaves grown in different position, hydrothermal reaction temperature, reaction time, and amount of mulberry leaves on fluorescence intensity of the prepared self-N-doped CDs were discussed. Single factor experiment was conducted by controlling variable method. In discussing the influence of one factor on the fluorescence intensity of the prepared self-N-doped CDs, other experimental conditions were controlled to be unchanged.

Excitation spectrogram of the prepared self-N-doped CDs from mulberry leaves which were collected in Hebei, Anhui, Guangxi, and Shandong province, China is shown in Fig. 1a. The fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs 3 is the strongest. In order to discuss the relationship between fluorescence intensity and nitrogen content, the protein content in mulberry leaves was determined by Kjeldahl method, and then the nitrogen content was calculated. The variation trend of fluorescence intensity was basically consistent with that of nitrogen content, and the conclusion can be clearly observed from Fig. 1b. The results showed that the content of nitrogen in mulberry leaves might be the main factor affecting the fluorescence intensity (Wang et al., 2020a, 2020b; Yang et al., 2016). The mulberry leaves 2 had high nitrogen content but the fluorescence intensity of the prepared self-N-doped CDs from mulberry leaves 2 is not high as that from mulberry leaves 3, which may be related to the polysaccharide content in mulberry leaves. The mass ratio of polysaccharides in mulberry leaves was 78.65–233.47 mg/g, and the purity of 99.8% mulberry leaves polysaccharide contained D-arabinose, D-xylose, D-glucose, D-rhamnose and D-mannose, etc. The molar ratio of these monosaccharides is 1:2.13:6.53:1.04:8.73 (Zhang et al., 2014). Almost all of these monosaccharides can be used as carbon sources to prepare CDs (Cheng et al., 2017; Egorova et al., 2018; Liao et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2019). On the same hydrothermal preparation conditions, the concentration of the prepared CDs will increase with the increase of the polysaccharides content of mulberry leaves, and it is speculated that the polysaccharide content in mulberry leaves will affect the fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs. The polysaccharides content of the mulberry leaves has relationship with the producing area of mulberry leaves and the local climate. Although the nitrogen content of the mulberry leaves 2 is high, the content of polysaccharide is not high due to the climate and the length of sunshine. The fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs will increase with both the concentration of CDs and doped N content of the self-N-doped CDs, so the fluorescence intensity of the prepared self-N-doped CDs from mulberry leaves 2 is not as high as that from mulberry leaves 3 (Guo and Hu, 2020).

The effect of mulberry leaves dosage on fluorescence intensity was further analyzed. Fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs was the strongest when 2.2 g mulberry leaves was added to 10 mL deionized water on the conditions of 200 °C for 12 h, which can be seen from Fig. A.1a (Appendix A. Supplementary data). The effect of hydrothermal reaction temperature on the self-N-doped CDs fluorescence intensity was further analyzed. As shown in Fig. A.1b, fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs prepared at hydrothermal reaction temperature of 200 °C was the strongest. The self-N-doped CDs were prepared at different reaction times, and the fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs obtained and presented in Fig. A.1c. The optimal hydrothermal reaction time is 14 h. The optimal hydrothermal reaction conditions of preparation for the self-N-doped CDs were as follows: Mulberry leaves 3 as carbon source and nitrogen sources, reaction temperature was 200 °C, reaction time was 14 h, mulberry leaves dosage was 2.2 g, and deionized water was 20 mL. The self-N-doped CDs number, mulberry leaves source, nitrogen content of mulberry leaves, reaction temperature, raw material dosage and reaction time are shown in Table 1.

The shape and particle size of the self-N-doped CDs were observed via HRTEM. The image in Fig. 2a and b obviously confirmed the shape and particle size of the self-N-doped CDs, which exhibited diameters from 2.23 nm to 5.22 nm, and the average diameter size was 3.84 nm. The self-N-doped CDs lattice spacing of 0.2 nm was observed under HRTEM (inset in Fig. 2a), which was consistent with the graphite-carbon plane (100) (Fu et al., 2021), confirming the existence of graphite-carbon. The self-N-doped CDs were characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2c, a wide diffraction peak was found in the range of 18°22°, which was consistent with the 002 plane of graphite (Liao et al., 2020). The wide diffraction indicated the presence of the disordered carbon in the structure of the self-N-doped CDs (Yang et al., 2021).

The functional groups of the self-N-doped CDs were analyzed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2d, the wide peak at 3430 cm 1 belongs to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H (Shi et al., 2019). These functional groups were hydrophilic, making the self-N-doped CDs soluble in water. The peaks at 2930 cm 1 and 2839 cm 1 were the result of C-H stretching vibration (Guo et al., 2021). The strong peak at 1600 cm 1 was formed by the superposition of CC skeleton stretching vibration and CN stretching vibration in aromatic hydrocarbons (Zhang and Fan, 2021), it means that the amino functional group is present in the structure of the self-N-doped CDs. The splitting peaks between 1400 cm 1-1500 cm 1 indicate the presence of aromatic rings (Yang et al., 2021). A typical band of C-H bending vibration was found at 1358 cm 1. The peak of 1174 cm 1 proved C-O stretching vibration (Liao et al., 2021). The peaks of 788 cm 1 and 590 cm 1 were caused by stretching vibrations of C-H in aromatic hydrocarbons (Yang et al., 2021). The above results indicate that the surface of the self-N-doped CDs contains abundant N and O functional groups.

In order to further analyze the chemical elements and surface chemical bonds of the self-N-doped CDs, XPS characterization analysis was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 3a, in the survey of XPS, there were three obvious peaks at 284 eV, 401 eV and 530 eV, representing C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The atomics percentage of C, N, and O were 73.47%, 12.86% and 13.58%, respectively. The types of chemical bonds on the self-N-doped CDs were studied in more detail in XPS spectra of single element fitting. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in Fig. 3b were at 284.7 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.8 eV, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-O/C-N and CO, respectively (Qi et al., 2019). The characteristic peaks appearing at 398.6 eV, 399.4 eV and 400.1 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s in Fig. 3c were assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and N-H, respectively (Yang et al., 2018). O1s high-resolution XPS atlas of the self-N-doped CDs was shown in Fig. 3d. It can be seen that CO (530.8 eV) and C-O (532.2 eV) exist (Gunjal et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2020, 2019). The analysis results of FT-IR and XPS were consistent, and these results indicate that the self-N-doped CDs are carbon and nitrogen rich nanoparticles with large amounts of NH2, COOH, and OH on the surface. The existence of COOH, NH2, and OH may be the reason for the good water-solubility of the self-N-doped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary study on the formation mechanism of the self-N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mulberry leaves contain the dry matter, 13.61%− 24.97% crude protein, 3.99%− 17.44% soluble sugar, 0.09%− 0.23% crude ash, 3.88% cellulose and 3.79% lignin, etc. These substances can be converted into CDs under suitable hydrothermal conditions. The protein in mulberry leaves is decomposed into amino acids under the condition of heating, which is the main component of N-doping. Soluble sugars and cellulose decompose into small molecules such as soluble oligomers and monosaccharides under hydrothermal conditions, and then dehydrate into soluble products, which form soluble polymers through condensation or polymerization reactions. With the increase of the reaction time, the structure of carbon network expands continuously. Organic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) such as lignin gradually fragment and finally polymerize through molecular self-assembly into carbon networks with specific groups. In conclusion, the main components of mulberry leaves are broken down into small molecules during hydrothermal processes, which form aromatic clusters through self-assembly, further form carbon cores, and finally form carbon dots with fluorescence (Ansi and Renuka, 2018; Xu et al., 2021; Zhang and Fan, 2021; Zhao et al., 2020). The fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs increased as temperature increase, and the maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved at 200 °C. When the temperature was higher than 200 °C, the fluorescence intensity begins to decrease, which may be caused by the formation of carbon quantum dot precursors with fluorophore when the temperature is lower than the nucleation temperature. When the temperature rises to a certain temperature, the carbon nuclei is gradually formed and the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots is gradually increased. Pyrolysis will occur when the temperature continues to rise. In the high-temperature pyrolysis process, the fluorophores formed at low temperature gradually turn into carbon nuclei. With the increase of temperature, the carbon nuclei size of CDs gradually increases, the surface fluorophore gradually decreases, and the fluorescence intensity also decreases (Zhao et al., 2020).

The relationship between the structure and the self-N-doped CDs is analyzed. The self-N-doped CDs prepared from mulberry leaves consist of carbon nuclei and surface functional groups. In the spectrum of the self-N-doped CDs, the excitation band at 350 nm may originate from the n-π * transition of the self-N-doped CDs surface functional group. The XPS spectra show that there is pyrrole N in the self-N-doped CDs structure, which makes the self-N-doped CDs produce surface defects. When the excitation light hits the self-N-doped CDs surface defect band, the electrons and holes are quantum-limited (particle size is 3.84 nm), and the continuous band structure becomes a discrete energy level structure with molecular characteristics. After excitation, the excitons will radioactively recombine and emit fluorescence (Liao et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the self-N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties are the focus of fluorescent probes. In this study, UV–Vis spectrum, emission spectrum, and excitation spectrum of the self-N-doped CDs were shown in Fig. 4a. The self-N-doped CDs have three shoulder bands absorption at 248 nm, 275 nm, and 320 nm, which was in its UV–Vis spectrum. The first two acromia were attributed to p-p * transition of CC, and the last one was produced by CO n-p * transition of the self-N-doped CDs (Chen et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2013; Yan et al., 2019). It can be seen from the Fig. 4a that the excitation peak position of the self-N-doped CDs was 350 nm and the emission peak position was 420 nm. It can be seen from inset in Fig. 4a, when the self-N-doped CDs were dissolved in water, it shows light yellow under sunlight and bright blue with the 365 nm UV lamp. The emission spectra were shown in Fig. 4b, the excitation wavelength is 320–450 nm with the excitation steps is 10 nm. It can be seen that the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 350 nm and 420 nm, respectively. The emission spectrum was dependent on excitation wavelength, and the maximum emission peak position of the emission spectrum gradually redshifts from 410 nm to 490 nm with the increase of the excitation wavelength. This phenomenon may be caused by particle size, surface defects and surface functional groups from the self-N-doped CDs (Al-Hashimi et al., 2020). After five repeated experiments, the QY of the self-N-doped CDs prepared in this paper is 9.3%, which was higher than the QY of CDs prepared from some other plant derived biomass as shown in Table 2, indicating that the nitrogen-containing mulberry leaves had certain advantages and significance in CDs preparation.

Next, fluorescent stability of the self-N-doped CDs was explored. As shown in Fig. A.2a, the fluorescence intensity decreased by only 0.92% within 7 days of storage and 7.58% within five months of storage. In the study of Fu et al. (Fu et al., 2021), the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs decreased by 5.6% compared with the first day after 7 days of storage. It is found that the self-N-doped CDs prepared in this work have better stability. The pH is an important factor affecting the fluorescence stability, which is also discussed in this study. It can be seen from Fig. A.2b, fluorescence intensity changes little when pH was 3–12, indicating that the fluorescence properties were less affected by pH and has strong stability. Compared with other fluorescent probes, the self-N-doped CDs prepared in this work can be applied to a wider range of acid-base environments (Gunjal et al., 2019; Sharma and Yun, 2020; Yu et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2020). The deionized water, which was used in later experiments, has a pH of 6.8. In addition, the change of fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp was also explored in the study. It can be seen from Fig. A.2c, The self-N-doped CDs aqueous solution was continuously irradiated with UV lamp at 365 nm, and the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly after 3 h, and the change of fluorescence intensity was negligible, indicating that the self-N-doped CDs had certain light stability (Shi et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021a, 2021b). The influence of salt ions on the stability of the self-N-doped CDs was explored. From Fig. A.2d, fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs does not change significantly with the increase of NaCl concentration from 0 M to 1.0 M, indicating that the self-N-doped CDs has a high salt tolerance, which was conducive to the actual sample detection in the presence of salt solution (Shi et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of TC based on the self-N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
When the concentration of TC increases from 0 μM to 40.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs (λ
ex = 350 nm) minished in order, as can be observed from Fig. 5a. As shown in Fig. 5b, the quenching efficiency of fluorescence intensity F/F
0 has a corresponding functional relationship with the concentration of TC. When the concentration of TC are between 0.158–2.0 μM (illustrated in Fig. 5b) and 2.0–40.0 μM, the quenching efficiency of the fluorescence intensity shows an linear relationship with TC concentration, which can be described as Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively.
(2)
F/F
0 = 100.20–8.90C r
2 = 0.998
(3)
F/F
0 = 84.57–1.35C r
2 = 0.997
where F is the fluorescence intensity after adding TC, F
0 is the fluorescence intensity before adding TC, and C is the concentration of adding TC. The results showed that the quenching efficiency of fluorescence intensity decreased with the concentration of TC increase between 2.0 and 40.0 μM. Next, the detection limit of the fluorescent probe was analyzed as LOD= 3δ/S (Shi et al., 2019), where δ is the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity of the blank self-N-doped CDs solution (n = 5), and S is the slope of the linear equation. According to the LOD= 3δ/S principle, the LOD of the fluorescence probe in this work was calculated to be 158 nM. As it can be seen from Table 3, compared with other fluorescent probes, national and local standards, the LOD of this probe is lower than that of existing fluorescent probes, but it is not yet comparable with that of standard methods.

Stability is an important criterion used to judge the performance of fluorescence sensor. In order to explore the stability of this fluorescent probe for TC detection, fluorescence scan experiment was carried out on the self-N-doped CDs solution containing TC (20.0 μM). As shown in Fig. 5c, fluorescence intensity remained basically stable, indicating that the detection system was relatively stable and suitable for detecting TC in actual samples (Moaddab and Ghasemi, 2021; Zhao et al., 2018). Selectivity is critical to the reliability of the analytical method, so the TC selectivity of the self-N-doped CDs probe was analyzed. Many possible interfering substances including SMS, CPL, ROX, AML, Na+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd+, Ca2+, K+, HCO3
, CO3 2–, HPO4 2–, H2PO4
, NO2–, SO4 2 , HA, SA and BPA with 2.0 M were added into the self-N-doped CDs solution. After 5 min, fluorescence intensity was measured with 350 nm excitation by fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence intensity was compared with the blank control group. According to Fig. 5d, fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs decreases when TC is added, while other possible interfering substances had no significant effect on it. The above results indicate that the as-prepared self-N-doped CDs have good stability and selectivity for TC detection.

A fluorescent test paper for TC detection based on the self-N-doped CDs was prepared. Fluorescent test paper has good stability over 50 days (Fig. 5e). When the test paper was immersed in 20.0 μM solution of various antibiotics (TC, SMS, CPL, ROX, and AML), the experimental phenomenon observed by UV lamp under 365 nm was that only TC darkened the color of the test paper (Fig. 5f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible TC detection mechanism based on the self-N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of fluorescence quenching by TC was discussed. As shown in Fig. 6a, TC has two wide absorption peaks at 274 nm and 358 nm, while the excitation and emission peaks of the self-N-doped CDs was at 350 nm and 420 nm, respectively. The peak from TC at 358 nm is overlaps with the peak from the self-N-doped CDs at 350 nm, but only partially overlaps with the peak from the self-N-doped CDs at 420 nm. Therefore, there may be an internal filtering effect (IEF) between TC and the self-N-doped CDs. Specifically, the interaction energy between the self-N-doped CDs and TC is absorbed by TC at the wavelength of 358 nm, so the fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs decreases and fluorescence quenching occurs. From Fig. 6b and c, before and after addition of 20.0 μM TC, the fluorescence lifetimes were 15.69 ns and 15.52 ns, respectively. According to the almost constant fluorescence lifetime value, the interaction between TC and the self-N-doped CDs may also be caused by static quenching; therefore, the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the self-N-doped CDs may be due to the formation of non-fluorescent complexes of ground-state the self-N-doped CDs with TC. Zeta potential is an important parameter, which can determine whether electrostatic adhesion accelerates the interaction between the prepared the self-N-doped CDs and TC. The Zeta potentials of the self-N-doped CDs before and after adding TC are 0.118 mV and − 1.53 mV, respectively, changing from positive potential to negative potential, indicating electrostatic adhesion effect. It is speculated that there may be electrostatic quenching effect. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of self-N-doped CDs induced by TC may be the result of IFE and static quenching (Feng et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in the river water
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of test the application of the as-constructed fluorescence probe in actual samples, the self-N-doped CDs was used to detect TC in the Yongjiang water by standard addition method. The experimental results show that the recovery rate of TC in the Yongjiang water was 98.6–102.2%, which is put in Table 4, and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) is no more than 2.54%. TC addition experiments in the Yongjiang water show that the self-N-doped CDs sensor has the ability to recognition of TC in the actual river water. The test paper was soaked in the actual river water with different concentrations of TC (0 μM, 0.1 μM, 1.0 μM, 10.0 μM, 20.0 μM, 30.0 μM, and 40.0 μM), and the blue fluorescence of different brightness could be displayed by UV lamp under 365 nm. When there was no TC, the fluorescence brightness reaches the maximum. With the increase of TC concentration, the brightness gradually dimmed, it can be seen from Fig. 7. The test paper can achieve visual and rapid detection results, and TC in the river water can be quickly detected with the as-prepared test paper.

In summary, the self-N-doped CDs was prepared from mulberry leaves by one-step hydrothermal method and used to construct fluorescent probe. The QY of the self-N-doped CDs prepared by this method is 9.3%. The probe has high stability, and significant selectivity for TC recognition. The TC test paper prepared on this basis can be identified by naked eye under 365 nm light illumination, and the LOD for TC of this fluorescent probe was calculated to be 158 nM. The recoveries of the actual samples were in the range of 98.6–102.2% and RSD of the actual samples was less than 2.54%. The above results show that the self-N-doped CDs based fluorescent probe was very effective for the rapid visual detection of TC, which was of great significance for water quality detection. In addition, the successful preparation of the self-N-doped CDs provides a novel strategy for the fluorescence enhancement of CDs and a new method for high-value-utilization of mulberry leaves.
